target.name: (-)-Aspidospermidine
target.motivation: Aspidospermidine is the parent compound of the largest subclass of Aspidosperma alkaloids and possesses the subclass’ characteristic ABCDE pentacyclic framework with four contiguous stereocenters. Its synthesis has become a proving ground for novel synthetic methodologies.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: enantioselective palladium-catalyzed decarboxylative allylation of carbazolones
reductive interrupted Fischer indolization followed by redox diversification

success_criteria.free_text: Shortest enantioselective synthesis to date (7 linear steps).

key_disconnections.named_strategy: aza-Michael addition and intramolecular enolate alkylation
key_disconnections.reasoning: The keto-group in 4-oxo-aspidospermidine (I) is a residual functionality of an α,β-unsaturated ketone (II), which serves as a bipolar functional handle for establishing both the D and E rings. This involves N-deprotection, aza-Michael addition, and intramolecular enolate alkylation from tricyclic endo-enone (II).
The endo-enone functionality (II) is established by an exo-to-endo double bond migration from exo-enone (III).

The exo-enone (III) is obtained from a key enantioselective allylic substitution reaction between a tryptamine derivative (V) and an allyl cation precursor (VI), followed by trapping of the indolenine (IV). This step constructs the stereodictating quaternary carbon stereocenter and assembles the entire framework.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy hinges on an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution as the stereo-defining step, followed by a diastereoselective sequence to construct the pentacyclic framework. This approach aims for a short and efficient synthesis of aspidospermidine.

thought.background: The retrosynthetic analysis identified tryptamine derivative V and allyl cation precursor VI as key starting materials for the allylic substitution. The authors needed to synthesize these substrates.
thought.gap: Synthesis of tryptamine derivative 7 and allyl cation precursors 9a-c.
thought.decision: To prepare the tryptamine derivative 7, 1-bromo-3-chloropropane and Boc2O were used. To prepare the Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a, acetaldehyde and DABCO were used. To prepare 9b, methyl chloroformate and 4-DMAP were used. To prepare 9c, PPh3, CH3COOH, and DIAD were used.

outcome.observation: Tryptamine derivative 7 was successfully synthesized.

thought.background: Substrates 7 and 9a-c have been prepared. The next step is the key enantioselective allylic substitution reaction.
thought.gap: Identify the best allyl cation precursor and optimize conditions for enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To identify the best allyl cation precursor, nonstereoselective allylic substitution protocols were submitted to 7 and 9a-c. Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a was selected as the best suited.

outcome.observation: Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a was selected as the best allyl cation precursor for the strategy.

evidence.text_span: These substrates were submitted to nonstereoselective allylic substitution protocols (see the Supporting Information).7a,c From these studies, the Morita−Baylis−Hillman adduct 9a was selected as the allyl cation precursor best suited for our strategy. 9a

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselectivity, (S,S)-L1 was screened as a chiral ligand with Et3B as borane additive at 50 °C.

outcome.observation: The reaction delivered the desired product 10 with excellent chemo- and regioselectivity, but low yield and moderate e.r. (19:81 R:S).

evidence.text_span:
From screening of chiral ligands (see the Supporting Information for more information), the DACH-naphthyl L1 and DACH-phenyl L2 were found to afford the enantioenriched indolenine 10 with comparable enantiomeric ratios (e.r.) (Table 1, entries 1 and 2). Interestingly, the ANDEN-phenyl ligand L3 performed least effective (Table 1, entry 6). All reactions delivered the desired product 10 with excellent chemo- and regioselectivity. Since the DACH-phenyl ligand L2 afforded the indolenine 10 in the highest yield, it was taken forward in the optimization studies. Increasing the steric bulk of the borane additive to 9-BBN-octyl was found to increase the e.r. (Table 1, entry 3).6a,7b,10 While lowering the temperature to room temperature was shown to increase both the yield and e.r. (Table 1, entry 4). However, further cooling to $4 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was detrimental. (Table 1, entry 5). Other solvents or reagent ratios led to inferior results (Table 1, entries 7−11). Extending the reaction time had no effect on the yield of indolenine 10 (Table 1, entry 12). Finally, the optimized enantioselective conditions were shown to be scalable with the indolenine 10 obtained in a yield of $4 2 \%$ and an e.r. of 91:9 (Table 1, entry 13). Initial optimization of the enantioselective

Table 1. Optimization of the Conditions for the Enantioselective Allylic Substitution Reactiona

entry	Ln	borane	T (°C)	yield (%)	e.r. (R:S)
1b	(S,S)-L1	Et3B	50	23	19:81

a Unless otherwise stated, all reactions were performed on a 0.10 mmol scale with 9a (2.0 equiv). b Additional 9a (2.0 equiv) was added after $1 5 \ \mathrm { h }$ ; reaction time of $^ { 4 8 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }$ .

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselectivity, (S,S)-L2 was screened as a chiral ligand with Et3B as borane additive at 50 °C.

outcome.observation: The reaction delivered the desired product 10 with excellent chemo- and regioselectivity, moderate yield and low e.r. (26:74 R:S). (S,S)-L2 gave higher yield than (S,S)-L1.

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To increase e.r., the steric bulk of the borane additive was increased to 9-BBN-octyl with (S,S)-L2 at 50 °C.

outcome.observation: Increasing the steric bulk of the borane additive to 9-BBN-octyl increased the e.r. (14:86 R:S), but the yield decreased.

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To increase both yield and e.r., the temperature was lowered to room temperature with (S,S)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl.

outcome.observation: Lowering the temperature to room temperature increased both the yield and e.r. (9:91 R:S).

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To further increase yield and e.r., the temperature was lowered to 4 °C with (S,S)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl.

outcome.observation: Further cooling to 4 °C was detrimental, resulting in lower yield and e.r. (30:70 R:S).

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.

outcome.observation: The ANDEN-phenyl ligand L3 performed least effectively, resulting in low yield and e.r. (37:63 R:S).

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To improve the reaction, the amount of 9a was increased to 4.0 equiv with (S,S)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl at room temperature.

outcome.observation: Increasing the amount of 9a to 4.0 equiv resulted in lower yield and e.r. (18:82 R:S).

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To improve the reaction, the solvent was changed to PhMe with (S,S)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl at room temperature.

outcome.observation: Reaction in PhMe resulted in no product formation.

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To synthesize the natural product (-)-aspidospermidine, the ligand was switched to (R,R)-L2. The catalyst loading was increased to 15 mol% (R,R)-L2 and 5 mol% Pd2(dba)3 with 9-BBN-octyl at room temperature.

outcome.observation: Using (R,R)-L2 with increased catalyst loading resulted in very low yield and e.r. (56:44 R:S).

evidence.text_span:
From screening of chiral ligands (see the Supporting Information for more information), the DACH-naphthyl L1 and DACH-phenyl L2 were found to afford the enantioenriched indolenine 10 with comparable enantiomeric ratios (e.r.) (Table 1, entries 1 and 2). Interestingly, the ANDEN-phenyl ligand L3 performed least effective (Table 1, entry 6). All reactions delivered the desired product 10 with excellent chemo- and regioselectivity. Since the DACH-phenyl ligand L2 afforded the indolenine 10 in the highest yield, it was taken forward in the optimization studies. Increasing the steric bulk of the borane additive to 9-BBN-octyl was found to increase the e.r. (Table 1, entry 3).6a,7b,10 While lowering the temperature to room temperature was shown to increase both the yield and e.r. (Table 1, entry 4). However, further cooling to $4 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was detrimental. (Table 1, entry 5). Other solvents or reagent ratios led to inferior results (Table 1, entries 7−11). Extending the reaction time had no effect on the yield of indolenine 10 (Table 1, entry 12). Finally, the optimized enantioselective conditions were shown to be scalable with the indolenine 10 obtained in a yield of $4 2 \%$ and an e.r. of 91:9 (Table 1, entry 13). Initial optimization of the enantioselective

Table 1. Optimization of the Conditions for the Enantioselective Allylic Substitution Reactiona

entry	Ln	borane	T (°C)	yield (%)	e.r. (R:S)
11g	(R,R)-L2	9-BBN-octyl	rt	12	56:44

a Unless otherwise stated, all reactions were performed on a 0.10 mmol scale with 9a (2.0 equiv). g (R,R)-L2 (15 mol $\%$ ) and $\mathrm { P d } _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { d b } \mathsf { a } ) _ { 3 }$ (5 mol %).

thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To improve the reaction, the reaction time was extended to 24 h with (R,R)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl at room temperature.

outcome.observation: Extending the reaction time to 24 h improved the e.r. to 90:10, but the yield remained moderate.

evidence.text_span:
From screening of chiral ligands (see the Supporting Information for more information), the DACH-naphthyl L1 and DACH-phenyl L2 were found to afford the enantioenriched indolenine 10 with comparable enantiomeric ratios (e.r.) (Table 1, entries 1 and 2). Interestingly, the ANDEN-phenyl ligand L3 performed least effective (Table 1, entry 6). All reactions delivered the desired product 10 with excellent chemo- and regioselectivity. Since the DACH-phenyl ligand L2 afforded the indolenine 10 in the highest yield, it was taken forward in the optimization studies. Increasing the steric bulk of the borane additive to 9-BBN-octyl was found to increase the e.r. (Table 1, entry 3).6a,7b,10 While lowering the temperature to room temperature was shown to increase both the yield and e.r. (Table 1, entry 4). However, further cooling to $4 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was detrimental. (Table 1, entry 5). Other solvents or reagent ratios led to inferior results (Table 1, entries 7−11). Extending the reaction time had no effect on the yield of indolenine 10 (Table 1, entry 12). Finally, the optimized enantioselective conditions were shown to be scalable with the indolenine 10 obtained in a yield of $4 2 \%$ and an e.r. of 91:9 (Table 1, entry 13). thought.background: The tryptamine derivative 7 and Morita-Baylis-Hillman adduct 9a are prepared. The goal is to perform an enantioselective Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution.
thought.gap: Optimize the chiral ligand and reaction conditions to achieve high enantioselectivity and yield.
thought.decision: To scale up the reaction, a 1.0 mmol scale was used with (R,R)-L2 and 9-BBN-octyl at room temperature for 15 h.

outcome.observation: The optimized enantioselective conditions were scalable, affording indolenine 10 in 42% yield and 91:9 e.r. (R:S).

evidence.text_span:
Finally, the optimized enantioselective conditions were shown to be scalable with the indolenine 10 obtained in a yield of $4 2 \%$ and an e.r. of 91:9 (Table 1, entry 13). Initial optimization of the enantioselective

thought.background: Indolenine 10 has been successfully synthesized with good enantioselectivity. The next step is to cyclize 10 to form tricycle 11.
thought.gap: Identify suitable basic conditions for cyclization.
thought.decision: To cyclize indolenine 10, t-BuOK was used in THF at -78 °C.

outcome.observation: Tricycle 11 was produced as a single diastereomer in good yield.

evidence.text_span: With the key Pd-catalyzed enantioselective allylic substitution successfully performed, we turned our attention to the next steps in the synthetic route (Scheme 3). Indolenine 10 was cyclized under basic conditions to produce tricycle 11 as a single diastereomer. Moreover, the allylic substitution and intramolecular Mannich reaction could also be performed as a one-pot process, albeit with diminished yield.

thought.background: Indolenine 10 has been successfully synthesized with good enantioselectivity. The next step is to cyclize 10 to form tricycle 11.
thought.gap: Explore a one-pot process for allylic substitution and intramolecular Mannich reaction.
thought.decision: To perform a one-pot process, the allylic substitution was followed by addition of t-BuOK.

outcome.observation: The one-pot process for allylic substitution and intramolecular Mannich reaction was successful, but with diminished yield compared to the two-step process.

evidence.text_span: With the key Pd-catalyzed enantioselective allylic substitution successfully performed, we turned our attention to the next steps in the synthetic route (Scheme 3). Indolenine 10 was cyclized under basic conditions to produce tricycle 11 as a single diastereomer. Moreover, the allylic substitution and intramolecular Mannich reaction could also be performed as a one-pot process, albeit with diminished yield.

thought.background: Tricycle 11 has been synthesized. The next step is the exo-to-endo double bond migration.
thought.gap: N-protection of the exo-enone 11 and subsequent 1,4-hydrosilylation to form silyl enol ether 13.
thought.decision: To achieve the exo-to-endo double bond migration, N-protection of 11 with Boc2O was performed, followed by 1,4-hydrosilylation using Rh2(OAc)4 and Et3SiH.

outcome.observation: Silyl enol ether 13 was obtained in 81% yield over two steps (N-protection and 1,4-hydrosilylation).

evidence.text_span: The ensuing exoto-endo double bond migration presented a significant challenge (see the Supporting Information). Eventually, it was found that N-protection of the exo-enone 11 followed by 1,4-hydrosilylation gave the silyl enol ether 13 in $8 1 \%$ over two steps.11

thought.background: Silyl enol ether 13 has been synthesized. The next step is to convert it to the N-Boc endo-enone 14.
thought.gap: Identify a suitable oxidation method for the silyl enol ether.
thought.decision: To convert 13 to 14, an IBX-mediated Nicolaou oxidation was performed using IBX in DMSO:CH2Cl2.

outcome.observation: N-Boc endo-enone 14 was furnished, along with N-Boc exo-enone 12, which could be resubmitted to the 1,4-hydrosilylation reaction.

evidence.text_span: A subsequent IBX-mediated Nicolaou oxidation furnished the N-Boc endo-enone 14 together with N-Boc exo-enone 12, which could be resubmitted to the 1,4- hydrosilylation reaction.12

thought.background: N-Boc endo-enone 14 has been synthesized. The next step is N-deprotection and concomitant aza-Michael addition to form tetracyclic product 15.
thought.gap: Identify suitable conditions for N-deprotection and aza-Michael addition.
thought.decision: To achieve N-deprotection and aza-Michael addition, trifluoroacetic acid was used in CH2Cl2.

outcome.observation: Tetracyclic product 15 was furnished in good yield.

evidence.text_span: $N .$ -deprotection and concomitant aza-Michael addition proceeded in the presence of trifluoroacetic acid, furnishing the tetracyclic product 15.

thought.background: Tetracyclic product 15 has been synthesized. The next step is the intramolecular enolate alkylation to install the final E ring in pentacycle 16.
thought.gap: Identify suitable basic conditions for intramolecular enolate alkylation.
thought.decision: To install the E ring, intramolecular enolate alkylation was performed using t-BuOK in THF.

outcome.observation: Pentacycle 16 was formed in good yield, with only the single desired diastereomer.

evidence.text_span: The final E ring in pentacycle 16 was installed by an intramolecular enolate alkylation using t-BuOK. Pleasingly, both the aza-Michael addition and intramolecular enolate alkylation reactions afforded only the single desired diastereomer, when performed over one- or two-steps, due to the high substrate-induced stereocontrol.

thought.background: N-Boc endo-enone 14 has been synthesized. The D/E ring closures can also be performed as a one-pot transformation.
thought.gap: Optimize conditions for the one-pot D/E ring closure.
thought.decision: To perform the one-pot transformation, deprotection was done with BF3•OEt2, followed by addition of t-BuOK for intramolecular enolate alkylation.

outcome.observation: The one-pot D/E ring closure afforded pentacycle 16 in 71% yield, which was higher than the yield of 56% over two separate steps.

evidence.text_span: Pleasingly, the D/E ring closures could also be performed as a one-pot transformation. In which, the deprotection was performed with $\mathrm { B F } _ { 3 } {  ullet } \mathrm { O E t } _ { 2 }$ and subsequent addition of t-BuOK induced the intramolecular enolate alkylation, affording pentacycle 16 in a yield of $7 1 \%$ from endo-enone 14—which was higher than the yield of $56 \%$ over two separate steps. Gratifyingly, both the aza-Michael addition and intramolecular enolate alkylation reactions afforded only the single desired diastereomer, when performed over one- or two-steps, due to the high substrate-induced stereocontrol.

thought.background: Pentacycle 16 has been synthesized. The final step is the Wolff-Kishner reduction to yield (-)-aspidospermidine (1).
thought.gap: Perform the Wolff-Kishner reduction.
thought.decision: To obtain (-)-aspidospermidine (1), a Wolff-Kishner reduction was performed using N2H4•H2O and Na in DEG.

outcome.observation: (-)-Aspidospermidine (1) was obtained in 54% yield. Interestingly, the unnatural enantiomer was obtained, indicating that the stereochemical results from Trost and co-workers could not be extrapolated to this system.

evidence.text_span: A final Wolff−Kishner reduction yielded $( - )$ -aspidospermidine (1). Interestingly, the unnatural enantiomer of aspidospermidine (1) was obtained; therefore, it was not possible to extrapolate the stereochemical results from Trost and co-workers to our system.7b

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated for the entire synthesis, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: This is the shortest enantioselective synthesis of aspidospermidine reported to date.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of steps including failed attempts and optimizations is not explicitly stated.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly mentioned failed attempts during optimization of the allylic substitution.

validation.comparison_to_prior: This synthesis represents the first application of a Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution with a 3-substituted indole derivative in the synthesis of Aspidosperma alkaloids. It is the shortest enantioselective synthesis of aspidospermidine reported to date, in seven linear steps, surpassing the previous shortest of eight linear steps.
validation.significance: The reported strategy provides a novel and efficient enantioselective route to aspidospermidine, a key Aspidosperma alkaloid. The Pd-catalyzed allylic substitution is a stereo-defining step, and the subsequent pentacyclic framework construction is fully diastereoselective. This methodology has potential for synthesizing other Aspidosperma alkaloids.

source.title: Total Synthesis of (-)-Aspidospermidine via an Enantioselective Palladium-Catalyzed Allylic Substitution Strategy.
